cards.title: The Five Most Common Troubleshooting Scenarios
cards.front: When a Facebook campaign underperforms, which 5 failure patterns should you diagnose against first?
cards.back:
Diagnose against these five recurring failure patterns, each with its own fix path:
- Zero conversions (work the Zero Conversions Diagnostic Checklist)
- CPAs are too high
- CTR is high but CPC is low, yet conversions don't follow
- Social shares are high but conversions are low
- Relevance score drops or is low

cards.title: Other Scenarios Worth Knowing
cards.front: Beyond the big five, what other troubleshooting scenarios should you be aware of?
cards.back:
Not covered in depth but worth knowing:
- Low click-through rate (CTR)
- High cost per click (CPC)
- High CPA with large target audiences
- Ads not getting impressions from Facebook
- CPA rising as you scale campaigns

cards.title: The 'Reach May Be Slightly Lower' Warning
cards.front: Facebook warns 'Your ad's reach may be slightly lower' — should you panic and rebuild the ad?
cards.back: No. It's just a heads-up (typically triggered by long copy or a disliked image), NOT a disapproval. Facebook won't disapprove the ad at this stage. Just submit it and see what happens — often it won't affect your impressions at all. After hitting 'Place order,' double-check in Ads Manager that the campaign is listed with no errors.

cards.title: Facebook Pixel Helper
cards.front: What free Chrome tool confirms your pixel is firing correctly across the funnel?
cards.back: Facebook Pixel Helper (Chrome extension) — verify the pixel fires, and that it's the CORRECT pixel, on every page of the funnel.
cards.example: Click the URL in every ad with zero conversions and confirm the pixel fires on each page.

cards.title: GTmetrix for Page-Load Issues
cards.front: Conversions are zero and you suspect the page itself — what tool tests for it?
cards.back: GTmetrix (https://gtmetrix.com) — test funnel URL load speed when conversions are zero and you suspect a page-load, caching, or other interference issue. If load speed is the problem, bring in a technical person to resolve the issues making URLs load slowly.

cards.title: Don't Make Income-Claim Hooks
cards.front: Why can a hook like 'make $30,000 in 30 days' get your ad in trouble?
cards.back: Don't make subjective income-claim hooks. Any hook promising a specific income — especially with a timeframe (e.g., 'make $30,000 in 30 days') — is very likely to cause trouble with Facebook policy. Even hooks like 'how to create a six-figure income as a coach' make a subjective income claim and may run into policy issues.
cards.example: Avoid: 'make $30,000 in 30 days' / 'how to create a six-figure income as a coach'

cards.title: Zero Conversions Diagnostic Checklist
cards.front: Campaign produced NOTHING after 24-48 hours — what's the in-order checklist to find the break?
cards.back:
Work through this in order:
- Verify the pixel is on EVERY page of the funnel (Facebook Pixel Helper)
- Verify it's the CORRECT pixel on every page
- Confirm you're optimizing for the right conversion event AND that reporting columns match
- Check that all funnel URLs are actually loading (GTmetrix)
- Check the landing pages — click every CTA button/link
- Confirm the landing page actually HAS a CTA button or link at all

cards.title: Verifying It's the CORRECT Pixel
cards.front: Pixel fires on every page — but how do you know it's even the RIGHT pixel?
cards.back: Clients can have multiple ad accounts/companies and multiple pixels on one site. Cross-reference: Ads Manager > All Tools dropdown > Pixels, find your Ad Account Pixel ID on the right side, and confirm it matches the Pixel ID shown by the Facebook Pixel Helper on every page.

cards.title: Optimization Event vs. Reporting Columns
cards.front: You ARE getting conversions but the report shows zero — what mismatch causes this?
cards.back:
The event you optimize for may not appear in the standard Ads Manager report. Confirm you're optimizing for the right conversion event AND that your reporting columns match:
- Verify the optimized event at the ad set level
- Customize report columns to show that conversion event
- Save the view as a named preset
- Set it as the default view

cards.title: Checking Landing Pages for Zero Conversions
cards.front: Pixel and event are fine but still zero conversions — what physical page checks remain?
cards.back:
Check the landing pages directly:
- Click every single CTA button/link and confirm it goes where it should
- Confirm the landing page actually HAS a CTA button or link at all (it has happened that a client wanted conversion traffic to a page with no button or link anywhere)

cards.title: High CTR / Low CPC but No Conversions: Three Culprits
cards.front: Your CTR is high and CPC is low — but no conversions. Which 3 things are to blame?
cards.back:
The problem is one of three things:
- Landing page — people click but don't convert; fix by split testing and reworking the landing/registration/sales page
- Ad scent — no continuity between the ad and the landing page; look at both side by side, find familiar components, and make them match
- Targeting — you've been sending unqualified traffic; re-examine targeting

cards.title: Ad Scent (Ad-to-Page Continuity)
cards.front: What is 'ad scent' and how do you fix it when clicks don't convert?
cards.back: Ad scent is the 'smell' or continuity between the ad and the landing page. When it's missing, people click but bounce. Fix it: look at the ad and the landing page side by side, check for similarities or familiar components, then make them match.

cards.title: Don't Assume an Interest Maps to Your Market
cards.front: Why might targeting people who 'like Oprah' still send you unqualified traffic?
cards.back: Don't assume an interest correlates with your market. Just because someone likes Oprah doesn't mean they read books. Make sure the interest actually correlates with — and maps to — your target market before targeting it.
cards.example: Liking Oprah does not mean someone reads books.

cards.title: High Social Shares but Low Conversions: Diagnostic Order
cards.front: Lots of likes, comments, and shares — but few conversions. What's the fix order?
cards.back:
High engagement doesn't guarantee conversions. Work through in order:
- Re-evaluate your campaign objective FIRST — Traffic or Page Post Engagement objectives deliver traffic/engagement, NOT conversions; switch to a conversion objective
- Look at your ad copy — is the hook strong, and does the copy lead to the next step? Make it crystal clear
- Rework your offer — high shares mean the hook resonates, so the offer is most likely missing the mark
- Re-examine ad copy and creative — the ad may be engaging people for the wrong reasons
cards.example: An ad with very high social shares wasn't converting; they revamped the copy, created a strong offer and call to action, and ADDED A URL in the copy — then it 'started converting like crazy.'

cards.title: Objective Drives What the Algorithm Delivers
cards.front: Why is the wrong objective the FIRST thing to check when shares are high but conversions are low?
cards.back: The algorithm delivers exactly what your objective asks for. If you're running a Traffic or Page Post Engagement objective, Facebook delivers traffic or engagement, NOT conversions. Re-evaluate your campaign objective first and switch to a conversion objective.

cards.title: Don't Assume High Engagement = Conversions
cards.front: What false assumption makes marketers celebrate shares while sales stay flat?
cards.back: Don't assume high engagement = conversions. High social shares, likes, and comments do not guarantee conversions. Often the objective is wrong (Traffic/Engagement instead of Conversions) or the offer is missing the mark.
